title: Acute Liver Failure
oneLiner: Rapid (<26 wks) severe hepatocyte injury causing jaundice, coagulopathy (INR>1.5), and encephalopathy in a previously normal liver – catastrophic without urgent management.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Cerebral oedema / raised ICP:** Falling GCS, pupillary asymmetry, Cushing’s reflex (bradycardia + hypertension) → **sign: papilloedema** on fundoscopy (late). This is not sepsis – you must treat with mannitol/hypertonic saline, not fluids.
- **Variceal haemorrhage (if underlying cirrhosis missed):** Haematemesis/melaena with haemodynamic instability → **sign: fresh blood on NG aspirate** – needs Terlipressin, antibiotics, OGD, not just lactulose.
- **Hypoglycaemia:** Impaired gluconeogenesis → **sign: dextrose stick <3.0 mmol/L** in an encephalopathic patient – treat with IV 10% glucose stat, before any other workup.
- **Sepsis (spontaneous bacterial peritonitis or cholangitis):** High fever, rigours, abdominal pain → **sign: neutrophils in ascitic fluid** – needs IV antibiotics (e.g. 3rd gen cephalosporin) within 1 hour, not just supportive care.
sections.Presentation:
- **Symptoms (3–5 cardinal):** Rapid-onset jaundice (days to weeks), confusion/drowsiness (hepatic encephalopathy), nausea/vomiting, right upper quadrant pain, easy bruising/bleeding.
- **Exam findings:** Jaundice (scleral first), fetor hepaticus (sweet/ammonia breath), asterixis (liver flap), Glasgow Coma Scale <15, hepatomegaly or shrinking liver ( poor prognosis), ascites, spider naevi (if chronic component).
- **Timing:** Distinguish acute (<8 wks from jaundice to encephalopathy) vs subacute (8–26 wks) – determines aetiology (viral vs drug vs indeterminate).
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Acute severe hepatitis (viral, drug, autoimmune):** Raised transaminases + low albumin – check IgM anti-HAV/HEV, acetaminophen levels, ANA/ASMA.
  - **Acute-on-chronic liver failure (ACLF):** De novo decompensation in known cirrhosis – history of alcohol/NAFLD, stigmata of chronic liver disease (caput, splenomegaly).
  - **Biliary obstruction (stones, tumour):** Dilated ducts on US, less encephalopathy initially – check bilirubin (direct) + ALP/GGT.
  - **Sepsis / shock liver (ischaemic hepatitis):** Hypotension, peak AST>5000+ALT, rapid drop after resuscitation – POCUS shows global hypokinesia.
  - **Wilson’s disease (young <40):** Coombs-negative haemolysis, Kayser-Fleischer rings, low serum caeruloplasmin – double-check with slit-lamp.
  - **Budd-Chiari (hepatic vein thrombosis):** Painful hepatomegaly + ascites, flow absent on Doppler US.
- **Splitter:** **INR >1.5 + encephalopathy** in a previously normal liver = ALF. If liver is shrunken/cirrhotic + ascites + portal hypertension = ACLF.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - **ABC assessment** – check airway, breathing, circulation; monitor Spo2, BP, GCS q1h initially.
  - **Capillary blood glucose** (hourly if encephalopathic).
  - **Urine dipstick** (for blood/protein in Wilson screen), **urine pregnancy test** (women of childbearing age – essential).
  - **ETCO2** (if intubated) to guide hyperventilation for raised ICP.
- **Bloods (urgent panels):**
  - **LFTs + INR:** ALT/AST (often >1000 U/L in paracetamol/ischaemia; lower in viral/hepatic failure), bilirubin (direct/indirect), ALP, GGT.
  - **Coagulation:** INR, APTT, fibrinogen.
  - **FBC + blood film** (for haemolysis, schistocytes in Wilson).
  - **Renal + electrolytes + PO4** (hypophosphataemia in paracetamol recovery).
  - **Arterial blood gas** (lactate, pH – prognostic).
  - **Lactate + ammonia** (ammonia >150 μmol/L linked to cerebral oedema risk).
  - **Toxicology:** Paracetamol level (even if unclear history – 4h post-ingestion), alcohol level, illicit drugs screen.
  - **Virology:** IgM anti-HAV, HBV (HBsAg, IgM anti-HBc), HEV, EBV, CMV, HSV (if immunocompromised).
  - **Autoimmune:** ANA, ASMA, IgG levels.
  - **Wilson:** Caeruloplasmin, copper, 24h urinary copper.
  - **Pregnancy test** (must be done – not optional).
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** **Abdominal ultrasound with Doppler** – assess liver size/texture, exclude biliary obstruction, hepatic vein/portal vein patency.
  - **Second-line:** CT abdomen (if US equivocal or suspected Budd-Chiari); CT head if GCS low (to exclude intracranial bleed, though CT often normal in cerebral oedema).
- **Special tests:**
  - **Liver biopsy** – rarely needed acutely, only if autoimmune/indeterminate and coagulopathy corrected (risk of bleeding).
  - **Transjugular liver biopsy** if coagulation uncorrectable.
  - **EEG** if non-convulsive status epilepticus suspected.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:**
  - **King’s College Criteria (KCC)** for prognosis / transplant need:
    - Paracetamol: pH <7.30 OR lactate >3.0 mmol/L (post-resusc) OR (INR >6.5 + creatinine >300 μmol/L + grade 3-4 encephalopathy)
    - Non-paracetamol: INR >6.5 (any grade encephalopathy) OR any 3 of: age <10/>40, bilirubin >300 μmol/L, INR >3.5, jaundice to encephalopathy >7 days, aetiology (drug/indeterminate).
  - **MELD score** (used for transplant listing UK) – calculators online.
- **Never-miss test:** **Pregnancy test** in any female 12-50 – missed pregnancy with HELLP/AFLP can be fatal; also affects management (avoid tetracyclines, use N-acetylcysteine).
sections.Management:
#### Immediate (first hour / ED)
- **Secure airway** if GCS <8 or falling – intubate with rapid sequence, avoid etomidate (adrenal suppression); use propofol or thiopental (raises ICP? – [check local policy]).
- **IV access** x2 widebore; send all bloods, type & screen.
- **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline or 5% glucose (if hypoglycaemic) – avoid hypotonic fluids (risk of hyponatraemia worsening cerebral oedema).
- **Treat hypoglycaemia:** 50 mL 50% glucose IV or 250 mL 10% glucose over 15 min.
- **N-acetylcysteine (NAC)** if paracetamol overdose suspected, regardless of time – standard 3-bag regimen (1st: 150 mg/kg in 200mL 5%D over 1h; 2nd: 50 mg/kg in 500mL over 4h; 3rd: 100 mg/kg in 1000mL over 16h). Continue until INR trending down. [Check local policy for dose adjustments in renal failure.]
- **IV antibiotics** if signs of sepsis (or unknown aetiology) – e.g. piperacillin-tazobactam or cefotaxime (post-blood cultures).
- **Terlipressin** if variceal bleed suspected (1-2mg IV q4-6h) – but avoid if known coronary disease.
- **Lactulose** – for hepatic encephalopathy (15-30 mL PO/PR q1-2h initially then titrate to 2-3 loose stools/day).
- **Raised ICP management** – elevate head of bed 30°, mannitol 0.5-1g/kg IV over 20min (if not anuric) or hypertonic saline (3% NaCl 150mL IV bolus). Monitor serum osmolality <320 mOsm/L.

#### Ward (first 24-72h)
- **Transfer to ICU/HDU** – all ALF patients need high-level monitoring (ICP monitoring may be considered).
- **Continue NAC** if initial INR >1.5 or known overdose – may run for 48-72h (some protocols continue until INR <1.5 or improving).
- **Monitor GCS, NEWS2, urine output hourly.** Check glucose q1-2h. **Check INR 6-hourly** – trend is critical.
- **Correct coagulopathy only if bleeding actively or before invasive procedure** – FFP 15 mL/kg, cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <1.0 g/L, platelets if <50×10⁹/L. Do NOT give vitamin K (except if warfarin-induced).
- **Renal support** – consider CVVHDF if worsening renal function or metabolic acidosis.
- **Enteral nutrition** – start early nasogastric feeds (low protein initially if encephalopathy severe, but maintain energy).
- **Prophylaxis:** Stress ulcer (PPI IV e.g. pantoprazole 40mg OD), VTE (low MW heparin if INR not >2.0 – controversial; [check local policy]).
- **Avoid sedatives, opiates, benzodiazepines** – can precipitate further encephalopathy.

#### Long-term / outpatient
- If patient recovers (no transplant needed): **lifelong follow-up** for liver fibrosis (screen for varices, HCC).
- Consider drug-induced aetiology: counsel patient to avoid hepatotoxins.
- **Liver transplant assessment** ongoing via national unit (King’s/Addenbrooke’s/Edinburgh).

#### Default Orders (if admitted)
- **Fluids:** 5% glucose + 0.45% saline at 1-2 mL/kg/h (avoid hypotonic). Monitor Na+ closely.
- **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (if INR <2.0 and no active bleeding). If INR >2.0, consider pneumatic compression devices.
- **Diet:** NBM initially if severe encephalopathy (aspiration risk); otherwise soft diet, avoid salt restriction.
- **Observations:** NEWS2 q1h, GCS q1h, blood glucose q1h initially. Once stable, q4h.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **N-acetylcysteine (NAC):** Standard 3-bag regimen as above. **Suspected paracetamol overdose:** give regardless of time; continue until INR <1.5. Hypersensitivity reaction (~10%) – stop temporarily, give antihistamines, rechallenge slower rate.
- **Lactulose:** 15-30 mL PO/PR q1-2h until 2-3 soft stools/day, then 15-30 mL BD-TDS. Monitor bowel frequency, avoid diarrhoea (electrolyte/loss).
- **Rifaximin (if intolerant to lactulose or persistent encephalopathy):** 550mg PO BD. [Check local policy for cost/availability.]
- **IV antibiotics (empirical):** Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV TDS, or cefotaxime 2g IV TDS + metronidazole if biliary cause. Adjust for renal function.
- **Terlipressin:** 1-2 mg IV q4-6h (variceal bleed). Contraindicated: ischaemic heart disease.
- **Mannitol (for raised ICP):** 0.5-1 g/kg IV over 15-20 min. Monitor serum osmolality (target 310-320 mOsm/L). Not if anuric.
- **Renal adjustment:** Many drugs (penicillins, cefotaxime, etc.) require dose reduction if CrCl <30. NAC dose unchanged in renal failure.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **NAC overdose rate:** Anaphylactoid reaction if given too fast. **Stop immediately if rash, wheeze, hypotension** – restart at slower rate after antihistamines (chlorphenamine 10mg IV).
  - **Mannitol without urine output check:** causes pulmonary oedema and hyponatraemia.
  - **Lactulose in acute abdomen:** risk of bowel perforation – never use if suspect surgical cause.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Cerebral oedema / raised ICP (leading cause of death)
  - Sepsis/SIRS (spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, pneumonia)
  - Bleeding (DIC, variceal, peptic)
  - Hypoglycaemia
  - Renal failure (hepatorenal syndrome, acute tubular necrosis)
  - Multi-organ failure
- **Chronic:**
  - Liver fibrosis/cirrhosis (if recovery incomplete)
  - Hepatic encephalopathy (persistent)
  - Portal hypertension
  - Hepatocellular carcinoma (very long term, rare in ALF)
sections.Prognosis:
- **Mortality:** Without transplant, ~50-90% depending on aetiology and severity. Paracetamol-induced ALF has better outcome (~20-30% survival without transplant) compared to viral/drug-induced (30-60%).
- **King’s College Criteria (KCC) positive** – urgent transplant assessment needed; survival ~90% with transplant versus <10% without.
- **Good prognostic features:** Rapid improvement in INR, normal pH, lactate <3.0, mild encephalopathy (grade 1-2), paracetamol aetiology, young age.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Start NAC immediately** in any unexplained ALF – it's cheap, safe, and may help non-paracetamol cases (antioxidant effect). Do not wait for toxicology results.
- **Check ammonia level** – high (>150 μmol/L) predicts cerebral oedema; consider lactulose and early ICP monitoring (if available).
- **Call transplant centre early** – aim for <24h of admission. Do not wait for full aetiology workup.
- **Daily arterial blood gas** – lactate and pH are key dynamic markers.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT give dexamethasone or mannitol before checking for hypoglycaemia and hypovolaemia** – can worsen outcome.
- **Do NOT give fresh frozen plasma just because INR is high** – unless bleeding or pre-procedure. FFP masks coagulopathy and delays transplant decisions.
- **Do NOT sedate with benzodiazepines or opiates** – will worsen encephalopathy and mask GCS changes.
- **Do NOT start lactulose in a patient with ileus or abdominal distension** – risk of bowel ischaemia.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
(Unlikely – almost all ALF patients require inpatient management. If mild hepatic injury with normal INR and no encephalopathy, and aetiology resolved, consider discharge with strict return criteria.)
- **Return if:** Any confusion, vomiting, jaundice worsening, bleeding (gums, nose, melaena), fever, reduced urine output.
- **Follow-up:** Hepatology clinic within 1 week; LFTs + INR every 2 days until stable.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/ICU immediately if:**
  - **GCS <8** (need intubation)
  - **INR >3.0** or rising despite NAC
  - **Lactate >3.0 mmol/L** post-resuscitation
  - **pH <7.30** (metabolic acidosis)
  - **Creatinine >300 μmol/L**
  - **New focal neurology / seizure** (cerebral oedema)
  - **Systolic BP <90** despite fluid challenge
  - **NEWS2 >7** or rising by 2
- **Call transplant team** (e.g. King’s Liver Unit 020 3299 9000) for all ALF fulfilling any KCC criterion, or if uncertain.

*Last updated: [current date] – verify local policies for NAC protocol, ICP monitoring, and transplant referral.*

rawMarkdown:
## Acute Liver Failure
**One-liner:** Rapid (<26 wks) severe hepatocyte injury causing jaundice, coagulopathy (INR>1.5), and encephalopathy in a previously normal liver – catastrophic without urgent management.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Cerebral oedema / raised ICP:** Falling GCS, pupillary asymmetry, Cushing’s reflex (bradycardia + hypertension) → **sign: papilloedema** on fundoscopy (late). This is not sepsis – you must treat with mannitol/hypertonic saline, not fluids.
- **Variceal haemorrhage (if underlying cirrhosis missed):** Haematemesis/melaena with haemodynamic instability → **sign: fresh blood on NG aspirate** – needs Terlipressin, antibiotics, OGD, not just lactulose.
- **Hypoglycaemia:** Impaired gluconeogenesis → **sign: dextrose stick <3.0 mmol/L** in an encephalopathic patient – treat with IV 10% glucose stat, before any other workup.
- **Sepsis (spontaneous bacterial peritonitis or cholangitis):** High fever, rigours, abdominal pain → **sign: neutrophils in ascitic fluid** – needs IV antibiotics (e.g. 3rd gen cephalosporin) within 1 hour, not just supportive care.

### Presentation
- **Symptoms (3–5 cardinal):** Rapid-onset jaundice (days to weeks), confusion/drowsiness (hepatic encephalopathy), nausea/vomiting, right upper quadrant pain, easy bruising/bleeding.
- **Exam findings:** Jaundice (scleral first), fetor hepaticus (sweet/ammonia breath), asterixis (liver flap), Glasgow Coma Scale <15, hepatomegaly or shrinking liver ( poor prognosis), ascites, spider naevi (if chronic component).
- **Timing:** Distinguish acute (<8 wks from jaundice to encephalopathy) vs subacute (8–26 wks) – determines aetiology (viral vs drug vs indeterminate).

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely → less likely:**
  - **Acute severe hepatitis (viral, drug, autoimmune):** Raised transaminases + low albumin – check IgM anti-HAV/HEV, acetaminophen levels, ANA/ASMA.
  - **Acute-on-chronic liver failure (ACLF):** De novo decompensation in known cirrhosis – history of alcohol/NAFLD, stigmata of chronic liver disease (caput, splenomegaly).
  - **Biliary obstruction (stones, tumour):** Dilated ducts on US, less encephalopathy initially – check bilirubin (direct) + ALP/GGT.
  - **Sepsis / shock liver (ischaemic hepatitis):** Hypotension, peak AST>5000+ALT, rapid drop after resuscitation – POCUS shows global hypokinesia.
  - **Wilson’s disease (young <40):** Coombs-negative haemolysis, Kayser-Fleischer rings, low serum caeruloplasmin – double-check with slit-lamp.
  - **Budd-Chiari (hepatic vein thrombosis):** Painful hepatomegaly + ascites, flow absent on Doppler US.
- **Splitter:** **INR >1.5 + encephalopathy** in a previously normal liver = ALF. If liver is shrunken/cirrhotic + ascites + portal hypertension = ACLF.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - **ABC assessment** – check airway, breathing, circulation; monitor Spo2, BP, GCS q1h initially.
  - **Capillary blood glucose** (hourly if encephalopathic).
  - **Urine dipstick** (for blood/protein in Wilson screen), **urine pregnancy test** (women of childbearing age – essential).
  - **ETCO2** (if intubated) to guide hyperventilation for raised ICP.
- **Bloods (urgent panels):**
  - **LFTs + INR:** ALT/AST (often >1000 U/L in paracetamol/ischaemia; lower in viral/hepatic failure), bilirubin (direct/indirect), ALP, GGT.
  - **Coagulation:** INR, APTT, fibrinogen.
  - **FBC + blood film** (for haemolysis, schistocytes in Wilson).
  - **Renal + electrolytes + PO4** (hypophosphataemia in paracetamol recovery).
  - **Arterial blood gas** (lactate, pH – prognostic).
  - **Lactate + ammonia** (ammonia >150 μmol/L linked to cerebral oedema risk).
  - **Toxicology:** Paracetamol level (even if unclear history – 4h post-ingestion), alcohol level, illicit drugs screen.
  - **Virology:** IgM anti-HAV, HBV (HBsAg, IgM anti-HBc), HEV, EBV, CMV, HSV (if immunocompromised).
  - **Autoimmune:** ANA, ASMA, IgG levels.
  - **Wilson:** Caeruloplasmin, copper, 24h urinary copper.
  - **Pregnancy test** (must be done – not optional).
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** **Abdominal ultrasound with Doppler** – assess liver size/texture, exclude biliary obstruction, hepatic vein/portal vein patency.
  - **Second-line:** CT abdomen (if US equivocal or suspected Budd-Chiari); CT head if GCS low (to exclude intracranial bleed, though CT often normal in cerebral oedema).
- **Special tests:**
  - **Liver biopsy** – rarely needed acutely, only if autoimmune/indeterminate and coagulopathy corrected (risk of bleeding).
  - **Transjugular liver biopsy** if coagulation uncorrectable.
  - **EEG** if non-convulsive status epilepticus suspected.
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:**
  - **King’s College Criteria (KCC)** for prognosis / transplant need:
    - Paracetamol: pH <7.30 OR lactate >3.0 mmol/L (post-resusc) OR (INR >6.5 + creatinine >300 μmol/L + grade 3-4 encephalopathy)
    - Non-paracetamol: INR >6.5 (any grade encephalopathy) OR any 3 of: age <10/>40, bilirubin >300 μmol/L, INR >3.5, jaundice to encephalopathy >7 days, aetiology (drug/indeterminate).
  - **MELD score** (used for transplant listing UK) – calculators online.
- **Never-miss test:** **Pregnancy test** in any female 12-50 – missed pregnancy with HELLP/AFLP can be fatal; also affects management (avoid tetracyclines, use N-acetylcysteine).

### Management
#### Immediate (first hour / ED)
- **Secure airway** if GCS <8 or falling – intubate with rapid sequence, avoid etomidate (adrenal suppression); use propofol or thiopental (raises ICP? – [check local policy]).
- **IV access** x2 widebore; send all bloods, type & screen.
- **IV fluids:** 0.9% saline or 5% glucose (if hypoglycaemic) – avoid hypotonic fluids (risk of hyponatraemia worsening cerebral oedema).
- **Treat hypoglycaemia:** 50 mL 50% glucose IV or 250 mL 10% glucose over 15 min.
- **N-acetylcysteine (NAC)** if paracetamol overdose suspected, regardless of time – standard 3-bag regimen (1st: 150 mg/kg in 200mL 5%D over 1h; 2nd: 50 mg/kg in 500mL over 4h; 3rd: 100 mg/kg in 1000mL over 16h). Continue until INR trending down. [Check local policy for dose adjustments in renal failure.]
- **IV antibiotics** if signs of sepsis (or unknown aetiology) – e.g. piperacillin-tazobactam or cefotaxime (post-blood cultures).
- **Terlipressin** if variceal bleed suspected (1-2mg IV q4-6h) – but avoid if known coronary disease.
- **Lactulose** – for hepatic encephalopathy (15-30 mL PO/PR q1-2h initially then titrate to 2-3 loose stools/day).
- **Raised ICP management** – elevate head of bed 30°, mannitol 0.5-1g/kg IV over 20min (if not anuric) or hypertonic saline (3% NaCl 150mL IV bolus). Monitor serum osmolality <320 mOsm/L.

#### Ward (first 24-72h)
- **Transfer to ICU/HDU** – all ALF patients need high-level monitoring (ICP monitoring may be considered).
- **Continue NAC** if initial INR >1.5 or known overdose – may run for 48-72h (some protocols continue until INR <1.5 or improving).
- **Monitor GCS, NEWS2, urine output hourly.** Check glucose q1-2h. **Check INR 6-hourly** – trend is critical.
- **Correct coagulopathy only if bleeding actively or before invasive procedure** – FFP 15 mL/kg, cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <1.0 g/L, platelets if <50×10⁹/L. Do NOT give vitamin K (except if warfarin-induced).
- **Renal support** – consider CVVHDF if worsening renal function or metabolic acidosis.
- **Enteral nutrition** – start early nasogastric feeds (low protein initially if encephalopathy severe, but maintain energy).
- **Prophylaxis:** Stress ulcer (PPI IV e.g. pantoprazole 40mg OD), VTE (low MW heparin if INR not >2.0 – controversial; [check local policy]).
- **Avoid sedatives, opiates, benzodiazepines** – can precipitate further encephalopathy.

#### Long-term / outpatient
- If patient recovers (no transplant needed): **lifelong follow-up** for liver fibrosis (screen for varices, HCC).
- Consider drug-induced aetiology: counsel patient to avoid hepatotoxins.
- **Liver transplant assessment** ongoing via national unit (King’s/Addenbrooke’s/Edinburgh).

#### Default Orders (if admitted)
- **Fluids:** 5% glucose + 0.45% saline at 1-2 mL/kg/h (avoid hypotonic). Monitor Na+ closely.
- **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD (if INR <2.0 and no active bleeding). If INR >2.0, consider pneumatic compression devices.
- **Diet:** NBM initially if severe encephalopathy (aspiration risk); otherwise soft diet, avoid salt restriction.
- **Observations:** NEWS2 q1h, GCS q1h, blood glucose q1h initially. Once stable, q4h.

### Drugs & Doses
- **N-acetylcysteine (NAC):** Standard 3-bag regimen as above. **Suspected paracetamol overdose:** give regardless of time; continue until INR <1.5. Hypersensitivity reaction (~10%) – stop temporarily, give antihistamines, rechallenge slower rate.
- **Lactulose:** 15-30 mL PO/PR q1-2h until 2-3 soft stools/day, then 15-30 mL BD-TDS. Monitor bowel frequency, avoid diarrhoea (electrolyte/loss).
- **Rifaximin (if intolerant to lactulose or persistent encephalopathy):** 550mg PO BD. [Check local policy for cost/availability.]
- **IV antibiotics (empirical):** Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV TDS, or cefotaxime 2g IV TDS + metronidazole if biliary cause. Adjust for renal function.
- **Terlipressin:** 1-2 mg IV q4-6h (variceal bleed). Contraindicated: ischaemic heart disease.
- **Mannitol (for raised ICP):** 0.5-1 g/kg IV over 15-20 min. Monitor serum osmolality (target 310-320 mOsm/L). Not if anuric.
- **Renal adjustment:** Many drugs (penicillins, cefotaxime, etc.) require dose reduction if CrCl <30. NAC dose unchanged in renal failure.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **NAC overdose rate:** Anaphylactoid reaction if given too fast. **Stop immediately if rash, wheeze, hypotension** – restart at slower rate after antihistamines (chlorphenamine 10mg IV).
  - **Mannitol without urine output check:** causes pulmonary oedema and hyponatraemia.
  - **Lactulose in acute abdomen:** risk of bowel perforation – never use if suspect surgical cause.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Cerebral oedema / raised ICP (leading cause of death)
  - Sepsis/SIRS (spontaneous bacterial peritonitis, pneumonia)
  - Bleeding (DIC, variceal, peptic)
  - Hypoglycaemia
  - Renal failure (hepatorenal syndrome, acute tubular necrosis)
  - Multi-organ failure
- **Chronic:**
  - Liver fibrosis/cirrhosis (if recovery incomplete)
  - Hepatic encephalopathy (persistent)
  - Portal hypertension
  - Hepatocellular carcinoma (very long term, rare in ALF)

### Prognosis
- **Mortality:** Without transplant, ~50-90% depending on aetiology and severity. Paracetamol-induced ALF has better outcome (~20-30% survival without transplant) compared to viral/drug-induced (30-60%).
- **King’s College Criteria (KCC) positive** – urgent transplant assessment needed; survival ~90% with transplant versus <10% without.
- **Good prognostic features:** Rapid improvement in INR, normal pH, lactate <3.0, mild encephalopathy (grade 1-2), paracetamol aetiology, young age.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Start NAC immediately** in any unexplained ALF – it's cheap, safe, and may help non-paracetamol cases (antioxidant effect). Do not wait for toxicology results.
- **Check ammonia level** – high (>150 μmol/L) predicts cerebral oedema; consider lactulose and early ICP monitoring (if available).
- **Call transplant centre early** – aim for <24h of admission. Do not wait for full aetiology workup.
- **Daily arterial blood gas** – lactate and pH are key dynamic markers.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT give dexamethasone or mannitol before checking for hypoglycaemia and hypovolaemia** – can worsen outcome.
- **Do NOT give fresh frozen plasma just because INR is high** – unless bleeding or pre-procedure. FFP masks coagulopathy and delays transplant decisions.
- **Do NOT sedate with benzodiazepines or opiates** – will worsen encephalopathy and mask GCS changes.
- **Do NOT start lactulose in a patient with ileus or abdominal distension** – risk of bowel ischaemia.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
(Unlikely – almost all ALF patients require inpatient management. If mild hepatic injury with normal INR and no encephalopathy, and aetiology resolved, consider discharge with strict return criteria.)
- **Return if:** Any confusion, vomiting, jaundice worsening, bleeding (gums, nose, melaena), fever, reduced urine output.
- **Follow-up:** Hepatology clinic within 1 week; LFTs + INR every 2 days until stable.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/ICU immediately if:**
  - **GCS <8** (need intubation)
  - **INR >3.0** or rising despite NAC
  - **Lactate >3.0 mmol/L** post-resuscitation
  - **pH <7.30** (metabolic acidosis)
  - **Creatinine >300 μmol/L**
  - **New focal neurology / seizure** (cerebral oedema)
  - **Systolic BP <90** despite fluid challenge
  - **NEWS2 >7** or rising by 2
- **Call transplant team** (e.g. King’s Liver Unit 020 3299 9000) for all ALF fulfilling any KCC criterion, or if uncertain.

*Last updated: [current date] – verify local policies for NAC protocol, ICP monitoring, and transplant referral.*
